title: Design Patterns: How to Think Like an Architect
excerpt: Learn how design patterns solve real problems and make systems easier to build and maintain.

body.value:
A design pattern is a proven way to solve a common problem in software.

It is not code. It is a structure you can apply when you recognize a situation.

Example: when your code depends too much on a database, you can use a Repository pattern.

Without patterns, developers solve the same problems again and again.

This leads to messy code, duplication, and hard-to-change systems.

Patterns give you a shared language. Instead of long explanations, you can say: "use Strategy".

flowchart LR
Problem --> Recognize
Recognize --> Pattern
Pattern --> CleanSolution
CleanSolution --> MaintainableSystem

Every pattern solves a specific type of problem.

You first recognize the problem. Then you apply the structure of the pattern.

Patterns usually define roles: interfaces, implementations, and interactions.

flowchart LR
Client --> Abstraction
Abstraction --> ImplementationA
Abstraction --> ImplementationB

• Using patterns without a real problem
  • Overengineering simple code
  • Forgetting that patterns have trade-offs

value.text: Patterns are tools. Use them when the problem matches. Not before.

title: Strategy Pattern: Replace If-Else with Clean Design
excerpt: Learn how to remove conditional logic and make your system flexible.

body.value:
Strategy pattern lets you change behavior without modifying the main class.

You move logic into separate classes called strategies.

Large if/else blocks grow over time.

They become hard to read, test, and extend.

Strategy removes this problem by isolating behavior.

flowchart LR
Order --> Strategy
Strategy --> SwedenTax
Strategy --> USTax
Strategy --> NoTax

You define an interface. Then multiple implementations.

The main class uses the interface, not the implementation.

The correct strategy is selected at runtime.

sequenceDiagram
Client->>Order: SetStrategy()
Order->>Strategy: Calculate()
Strategy-->>Order: Result

• Too many small strategies
  • Choosing strategy in wrong place
  • Not using dependency injection

value.text: Use Strategy when behavior changes based on conditions.

title: Command Pattern: Turn Actions into Objects
excerpt: Encapsulate operations and unlock undo, queues, and flexibility.

body.value:
Command pattern wraps an action inside an object.

This allows you to execute, store, and undo operations.

flowchart LR
Client --> Invoker
Invoker --> Command
Command --> Receiver

In real systems, actions are not just executed once.

You may need to log them, retry them, or undo them.

sequenceDiagram
Client->>Invoker: Execute(Command)
Invoker->>Command: Execute()
Command->>Receiver: Action()

• Putting too much logic in invoker
  • Not storing command state

value.text: Command is perfect when actions must be stored, replayed, or undone.

title: Null Object Pattern: Eliminate Null Checks
excerpt: Write safer code without endless null checks.

body.value: Instead of returning null, return a safe default object.

Null checks increase complexity and bugs.

This pattern removes them.

flowchart LR
Service --> RealObject
Service --> NullObject
• Using null object where null has meaning

value.text: Replace null with behavior, not checks.

title: Specification Pattern: Keep Business Rules Clean

body.value: Specification pattern stores business rules in reusable objects.

flowchart LR
SpecA --> Combine
SpecB --> Combine
Combine --> Query

value.text: Put rules in one place and reuse them everywhere.

title: State Pattern: Replace Complex Conditions
excerpt: Manage object behavior using states instead of flags.

body.value: State pattern lets objects change behavior depending on their state.

stateDiagram-v2
[*] --> New
New --> Pending
Pending --> Completed
Pending --> Failed
Completed --> Closed
Failed --> New

value.code:
interface IState { void Handle(); }
class PendingState : IState { public void Handle() { } }

value.text: Replace flags and conditionals with state objects.
